2003.assessment:
Malaysia rated Partly Free by Freedom House with a Civil Liberties score of 5 (on
FH 1-7 scale). Press freedom constrained by Printing Presses and Publications Act
and media licensing requirements. ISA (Internal Security Act) allowed detention
without trial. Freedom of assembly restricted; racial and religious speech laws applied.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2004.assessment:
Under Badawi's reform rhetoric, FH rated Malaysia's civil liberties marginally
better at 4. Press still subject to licensing but some improvement in operating
environment. ISA remained on the books. Online news portal Malaysiakini growing
as alternative media. Partly Free classification maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2005.assessment:
Civil liberties remained at CL 4 (Partly Free). Internet growing as alternative
media space with less restriction than print. ISA continued with detainees.
Freedom of religion constrained particularly for non-Muslims in conversion cases.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2006.assessment:
Civil liberties score remained at 4. Freedom of religion controversies continued
(Lina Joy case). Internet media growing but print media licensing maintained.
Trade union rights restricted. Racial and religious speech laws actively enforced.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2007.assessment:
Civil liberties score 4; BERSIH rally dispersed by police with tear gas and
chemical-laced water. Hindraf march also forcibly dispersed and leaders detained
under ISA. Restrictions on assembly demonstrated in practice. Civil society
mobilizing despite constraints.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2008.assessment:
CL score 4 maintained. Opposition won 5 state governments in March 2008,
creating more pluralistic information environment. Internet freedom continued
expanding. ISA still used but overall civil liberties constrained by legal
framework and enforcement.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2009.assessment:
CL score 4. Najib's government maintained inherited civil liberties environment.
Teoh Beng Hock death during MACC custody raised serious due process concerns.
Some ISA detainees released but overall framework unchanged.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2010.assessment:
CL score 4. Moderate civil liberties environment with persistent structural
constraints. Social media becoming increasingly important as alternative news
source, partially circumventing traditional media restrictions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2011.assessment:
CL score 4. BERSIH 2.0 rally (July) involved mass civil disobedience followed by
police crackdown with tear gas and water cannons. ISA remained in use. Despite
restrictions, tens of thousands mobilized showing resilient civil society.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Civil Liberties Score for Malaysia

2012.assessment:
CL score 4. ISA replaced by SOSMA (Security Offences Special Measures Act),
which maintained detention powers. Peaceful Assembly Act formalized protest
restrictions. Overall civil liberties framework not significantly improved
despite nominal reform legislation.

CL score 4. Post-election period saw Najib blame 'Chinese tsunami' for BN losses,

CL score 4. Wave of sedition prosecutions against academics, lawyers, and opposition

CL score worsened to 5 (Not Free territory). 1MDB scandal triggered political

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet published for civil_liberties. Malaysia stable; expected to show continuity with 2024 values when released.

2003.assessment:
Mahathir Mohamad handed power to Abdullah Ahmad Badawi in October 2003 in an
orderly transition within UMNO. BN coalition remained unified. Elite cohesion
was high as the transition was planned and accepted by the UMNO party structure.

sources.citation: Bridget Welsh and James Chin, eds., 'Awakening: The Abdullah Badawi Years in Malaysia', 2013

2004.assessment:
BN under Badawi won a historic landslide in 2004 elections. Elite cohesion
high; Badawi enjoyed strong support within UMNO and BN coalition. No significant
factional challenges to Badawi's leadership.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Malaysia

2005.assessment:
Badawi's reform momentum stalled; UMNO internal tensions began over his son-in-law
Khairy Jamaluddin's rising influence. Minor tensions within BN but coalition
remained essentially unified with no serious factional challenges.

sources.citation: Reuters, 'Malaysian PM faces growing criticism', 2005

2006.assessment:
Growing UMNO internal dissatisfaction with Badawi. Mahathir Mohamad began openly
criticizing Badawi, especially over the crooked bridge project and other policy
reversals. BN coalition still functional but elite tensions rising.

sources.citation: Maznah Mohamad, 'The Paradox of Two Transitions in Malaysia', 2010

2007.assessment:
Elite tensions within UMNO intensified as Mahathir continued criticizing Badawi
and even resigned from UMNO briefly. BERSIH and Hindraf movements showed civil
society mobilization. Internal BN elite competition managed but increasingly visible.

sources.citation: Bridget Welsh, 'Malaysia's Political Transition', ISEAS 2008

2008.assessment:
March 2008 elections produced BN's worst result since independence, triggering
immediate elite pressure on Badawi to resign. UMNO divisions between Badawi and
Najib camps became open. Elite cohesion within ruling bloc severely weakened.

sources.citation: Meredith Weiss, 'Protest and Possibilities: Civil Society and Coalitions for Political Change in Malaysia', 2011

2009.assessment:
Badawi resigned as PM and UMNO president in April 2009; Najib Razak took over.
Transition was orderly within UMNO structure. Najib consolidated power relatively
quickly. BN coalition reconsolidated under new leadership. Elite cohesion restored
under Najib.

sources.citation: Reuters, 'Malaysia's Najib Takes Power from Badawi', April 2009

2010.assessment:
Najib consolidated power within UMNO and BN. Elite cohesion stable under new
leadership. 1Malaysia branding initiative had broad BN buy-in. Anwar Ibrahim's
opposition coalition was the main challenge but BN elites remained unified.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Malaysia

2011.assessment:
Najib's UMNO leadership stable; BN coalition preparing for elections. Minor
internal UMNO tensions over reformist agenda versus traditional base. Elite
cohesion maintained with clear Najib dominance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Malaysia

2012.assessment:
BN elite cohesion maintained ahead of 2013 elections. Najib pursued economic
transformation agenda with BN buy-in. Internal UMNO competition managed through
UMNO divisional elections, but party remained unified behind PM.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Malaysia

2013.assessment:
BN retained government in May 2013 despite losing popular vote. BN elites
cohesive in defending the result and government formation. Najib's position
secure; no significant internal challenge to his leadership despite narrow
victory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Malaysia

2014.assessment:
Elite cohesion maintained. Mahathir Mohamad increasingly critical of Najib's
1MDB-related decisions but remained within BN framework. Elite concerns about
1MDB beginning to circulate privately but no open factional break.

sources.citation: Terence Gomez, 'UMNO and the 1MDB Scandal', 2016

2015.assessment:
1MDB scandal caused major elite fractures. Mahathir openly attacked Najib;
Deputy PM Muhyiddin Yassin questioned 1MDB publicly and was fired in July 2015.
UMNO experienced open factional conflict between Najib loyalists and critics.
Despite conflict, Najib managed to purge critics and maintain formal party control.

sources.citation: BBC News, 'Malaysia PM fires deputy and attorney general', July 2015
Reuters, 'Mahathir calls for Najib's resignation', 2015

2016.assessment:
Elite fragmentation deepened. Mahathir Mohamad left UMNO and began organizing
formal opposition. Citizens' Declaration signed by former UMNO leaders, Mahathir,
and opposition figures calling for Najib's removal. Najib maintained formal UMNO
control but faced unprecedented elite defection.

sources.citation: Reuters, 'Malaysia: Mahathir leads 'Save Malaysia' coalition', 2016

2017.assessment:
Elite fragmentation at its peak in pre-election year. Mahathir founded PPBERSATU
as a new Malay party to challenge UMNO. Alliance with opposition PKR and DAP
formed Pakatan Harapan. Former UMNO stalwarts, former ministers, and even
previous PM's son Mukhriz Mahathir joined opposition. Najib's UMNO held
together through patronage but faced serious elite defection.

sources.citation: Bridget Welsh, 'Buying Time: The Politics of Patronage in Malaysia', 2017

2018.assessment:
Elite conflict resolved through elections: BN/UMNO defeated on May 9; Mahathir
became PM at age 93. Pakatan Harapan coalition nominally unified but internally
diverse. Post-election: BN elite in disarray; UMNO president Ahmad Zahid under
investigation; Najib arrested. PH coalition fraught with tension between
Mahathir, Anwar, and PKR factions.

sources.citation: Bridget Welsh, 'Democratisation in Malaysia: The State of Play after GE14', ISEAS 2018

2019.assessment:
PH coalition governed under significant internal tensions between Mahathir's
PPBERSATU/PKR factions and the agreed succession plan for Anwar to take over
as PM. Mahathir delayed transition; PKR faction frustrated. UMNO/BN in opposition
rebuilding but not yet cohesive.

sources.citation: ISEAS Yusof Ishak Institute, 'Malaysia's Contested Democracy', 2019

2020.assessment:
The Sheraton Move (February 2020) represented complete elite fragmentation:
PKR defectors, PPBERSATU, and UMNO formed a new coalition to collapse PH.
Muhyiddin Yassin became PM heading a minority government dependent on UMNO
support. Elite alignments unstable and shifting; constant defections and
counter-maneuvers throughout 2020.

sources.citation: Khoo Boo Teik, 'The Sheraton Move and the Political Economy of Malaysia's Instability', 2020
ICG, 'Navigating Change in Malaysia', 2020

2021.assessment:
Muhyiddin's government lost its majority and he resigned in August 2021.
Ismail Sabri formed government with UMNO support without election. Elite
instability continued with shifting alliances. Elite cohesion at a historic
low for modern Malaysia.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Malaysia

2022.assessment:
November 2022 elections produced hung parliament with no clear majority.
After tense negotiations, Anwar Ibrahim formed unity government including
former rivals. Elite competition managed through formal coalition building.
UMNO/BN joining Anwar government was surprising but stabilizing.

sources.citation: Reuters, 'Malaysia's Anwar forms unity government with BN', November 2022

2023.assessment:
Anwar unity government maintained but with ongoing elite tensions. State elections
showed competition between unity coalition components and PN opposition. Najib
imprisoned. Elite cohesion within government broadly maintained though with
visible rivalries.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Malaysia

2024.assessment:
Unity government broadly stable under Anwar Ibrahim. Minor tensions between
coalition partners managed. PN remained a credible opposition. Elite cohesion
within governing coalition adequate for governance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Malaysia

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet published for elite_cohesion. Malaysia stable; expected to show continuity with 2024 values when released.

2003.assessment:
Under Mahathir's final year, Malaysia's institutions functioned but under
significant executive dominance. Courts nominally independent but subject to
executive pressure since the 1988 judicial crisis. Elections held regularly
but with major structural advantages for BN through malapportionment and media control.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004, Malaysia

2004.assessment:
Abdullah Ahmad Badawi's government brought initial reform hopes. Parliament functional
with BN landslide majority; institutions operated but electoral commission and
judiciary remained closely tied to executive. Anti-corruption moves announced.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Malaysia

2005.assessment:
Institutions functioned under established BN framework. Parliament, bureaucracy,
and courts operational. Executive retained extensive influence over judiciary and
media. Elections competitive at surface level but structurally unfair.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Malaysia

2006.assessment:
Badawi's reform momentum stalled; UMNO internal politics dominated governance.
Elections machinery intact but gerrymandering entrenched BN advantage. Judiciary
showed limited independence in high-profile cases.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Malaysia

2007.assessment:
Increasing civil society pressure via BERSIH movement challenged electoral
commission credibility. Parliament functioned with BN supermajority. Courts
handled routine matters but politically sensitive cases saw executive influence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Malaysia

2008.assessment:
March 2008 elections marked a partial institutional opening as opposition won
5 state governments, demonstrating that the electoral system could produce
opposition victories despite structural disadvantages. Institutional functioning
improved at state level; federal institutions remained BN-controlled.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Malaysia

2009.assessment:
Najib Razak took over as PM. MACC (anti-corruption commission) established but
questioned for independence following the death of Teoh Beng Hock during MACC
custody. Institutions functioned but with integrity concerns.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Malaysia

2010.assessment:
Institutions functional in executive-dominated framework. ISA continued use
against political opponents. Courts operational but politically sensitive cases
saw executive influence.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Malaysia

2011.assessment:
BERSIH 2.0 demonstrated continued civil society challenge to electoral
institutions. Parliament functioned with significant BN dominance.
Anti-sedition and media licensing laws constrained political expression.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Malaysia

2012.assessment:
Some reforms: ISA replaced by SOSMA; Peaceful Assembly Act passed (though
restrictive). Elections Commission reforms partially conceded. Overall
institutional quality remained flawed with executive dominance.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Malaysia

2013.assessment:
May 2013 elections conducted under structural BN advantages. Opposition won
popular vote (51%) but BN retained government due to malapportionment.
International observers noted irregularities. Institutions functioned but
with significant democratic deficits.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2014, Malaysia

2014.assessment:
Institutional quality degraded with return to sedition prosecutions and
increased use of restrictive laws. 1MDB scandal began emerging, raising
questions about institutional oversight capacity. Judiciary remained
formally functional.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Malaysia

2015.assessment:
The 1MDB scandal revelations severely undermined institutional credibility.
AG Abdul Gani Patail removed during 1MDB investigation and replaced.
Oversight bodies (MACC, Bank Negara, police) seen as co-opted or suppressed.
Institutions formally functioning but oversight function severely compromised.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Malaysia

2016.assessment:
Institutional degradation continued. Parliament continued sitting but critical
questions suppressed; laws used to silence opposition. New AG declared no
wrongdoing re 1MDB. International investigations (DOJ, Swiss AG) proceeded
while Malaysian institutions failed to act independently.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Malaysia

2017.assessment:
Electoral redelineation process further skewed electoral system in BN's favor.
Anti-Fake News Act and other restrictive legislation prepared. Institutions
functioned formally but with deep integrity problems.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Malaysia

2018.assessment:
May 9 election demonstrated remarkable institutional resilience: despite all
structural advantages for BN, the Electoral Commission conducted an election
that BN lost. Post-election, PH began institutional reform; new AG appointed,
1MDB investigations reopened. Significant institutional improvement in H2 2018.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Malaysia

2019.assessment:
PH government pursued institutional reforms with limited success due to coalition
fragility. Najib Razak on trial demonstrating judicial accountability improvement.
Parliament active with opposition presence. Media freedom expanded somewhat.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Malaysia

2020.assessment:
The February 2020 Sheraton Move exploited constitutional ambiguities to collapse
the elected PH government without an election. Institutions failed to protect the
elected government. Muhyiddin's PN government had questionable democratic legitimacy.
Institutional functioning severely impaired.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Malaysia

2021.assessment:
Parliament suspended under emergency declaration (January-August 2021).
Muhyiddin resigned in August; replaced by Ismail Sabri without election.
Emergency rule bypassed normal legislative oversight. Institutional
quality significantly degraded from 2018-2019 improvement.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Malaysia

2022.assessment:
November 2022 elections competitive and functional. Hung parliament formation
of Anwar unity government. Institutional recovery began. Najib sentenced to
prison for SRC case demonstrating judicial accountability. Institutional
functioning improved from 2020-2021 low.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Malaysia

2023.assessment:
Anwar unity government pursued institutional strengthening. Parliament active.
Anti-corruption efforts continued. Institutional quality improving though
structural issues (press licensing, sedition laws) remain.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Malaysia

2024.assessment:
Institutional quality under Anwar unity government continued on recovery
trajectory. State elections competitive. Courts showed independence in
several rulings. Persistent challenges include selective prosecution concerns
and media licensing.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Malaysia

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet published for institutional_functioning. Malaysia stable; expected to show continuity with 2024 values when released.

2003.assessment:
Malaysia experienced no armed conflict or significant political violence in 2003.
The country was peaceful under BN rule with normal policing but no war-related
deaths or major terrorist incidents.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2003

2004.assessment:
No political violence or armed conflict in Malaysia in 2004. The landslide BN
election victory in March 2004 was peaceful. Malaysia remained one of Southeast
Asia's most stable states.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2004

2005.assessment:
Malaysia experienced no armed conflict or significant political violence. Normal
policing and security operations continued without war-related casualties.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2005

2006.assessment:
No significant political violence or armed conflict in Malaysia. Peaceful year
with no conflict-related deaths recorded in international datasets.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2006

2007.assessment:
No political violence. The BERSIH 1.0 rally in November 2007 involved police use
of tear gas and water cannons against protesters but resulted in no fatalities.
This was state repression of protesters rather than armed conflict.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2007
Human Rights Watch, 'Malaysia: Crackdown on Peaceful Protesters', November 2007

2008.assessment:
No armed political violence. The March 2008 elections produced a major political
upset but were conducted peacefully. No conflict deaths recorded.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2008

2009.assessment:
No armed conflict or significant political violence. Najib Razak's accession to
PM in April 2009 was a smooth intra-party transition without violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2009

2010.assessment:
No armed conflict. Malaysia remained peaceful. Some incidents of police dispersal
of protests but no fatalities related to political conflict.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2010

2011.assessment:
No armed conflict deaths. BERSIH 2.0 rally (July 2011) saw police dispersal
of tens of thousands of protesters with tear gas and chemical-laced water, but
no fatalities. Political tension elevated but no armed violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2011
Amnesty International, 'Malaysia: Crackdown on BERSIH protesters', July 2011
UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2012

2013.assessment:
The February-March 2013 Lahad Datu standoff in Sabah involved armed followers of
the Sulu Sultanate from the Philippines; Malaysian security forces killed approximately
56 Sulu fighters and 9 Malaysian security personnel. This was an isolated incident
concentrated in a small area of eastern Sabah; not an ongoing insurgency.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2013
Reuters, 'Malaysia sends troops after Philippine gunmen kill policemen in Sabah', March 2013

2014.assessment:
No significant armed conflict. Post-Lahad Datu security was enhanced in eastern
Sabah. The disappearance of MH370 (March 2014) and shooting down of MH17 (July 2014)
were aviation disasters, not political violence within Malaysia.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2014

2015.assessment:
No armed conflict in Malaysia. Political tensions over 1MDB scandal were managed
through authoritarian legal measures (detentions under SOSMA, sedition law) rather
than violence. No conflict deaths recorded.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2015

2016.assessment:
No armed political violence. The political opposition and civil society continued
mobilizing against Najib but through legal and electoral channels. No conflict-related
deaths in Malaysia proper.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2016

2017.assessment:
No armed conflict. Malaysia prepared for the 2018 elections with intense political
maneuvering. The assassination of Kim Jong-nam at KLIA in February 2017 was an
isolated foreign-state act, not domestic political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2017

2018.assessment:
No armed political violence during the historic 2018 election that ousted BN after
61 years. The transition of power was entirely peaceful; no conflict deaths recorded.
Malaysia's regime change was a model of electoral transition.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2018

2019.assessment:
No armed conflict under the Pakatan Harapan government. Political tensions existed
around implementation of reform agenda and racial/religious politics, but no
political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2019

2020.assessment:
No armed political violence despite the February 2020 Sheraton Move political crisis
that toppled the PH government. Political instability was managed through parliamentary
maneuvering rather than violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2020

2021.assessment:
No armed conflict. Political instability continued as Muhyiddin's government
collapsed in August 2021. All transitions managed through constitutional and
parliamentary means; no political violence.

sources.citation: UCDP Georeferenced Event Dataset (GED), version 23.1, Malaysia, 2021

2022.assessment:
No political violence. November 2022 elections and subsequent formation of Anwar
Ibrahim unity government were conducted without violence. Malaysia continued as a
stable, violence-free political environment.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Malaysia, 2022, accessed 2026-03-01

2023.assessment:
No political violence in Malaysia. State elections in several states were conducted
peacefully. Malaysia remained one of Southeast Asia's most politically stable countries
in terms of physical security.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Malaysia, 2023, accessed 2026-03-01

2024.assessment:
No armed political violence in Malaysia. The Anwar Ibrahim unity government
consolidated. No conflict-related deaths reported.

sources.citation: ACLED Data for Malaysia, 2024, accessed 2026-03-01

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet published for political_violence. Malaysia stable; expected to show continuity with 2024 values when released.

2003.assessment:
Malaysia exercised full central government control over its territory under the
Barisan Nasional government. No armed insurgencies or territorial challenges
existed. Peninsular Malaysia, Sabah, and Sarawak all under effective federal administration.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2004, Malaysia

2004.assessment:
Full government territorial control maintained. BN under Abdullah Ahmad Badawi
won a landslide in the 2004 general elections. No armed groups challenged government
authority; minor cross-border smuggling in Sabah did not constitute a territorial challenge.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2005, Malaysia

2005.assessment:
Malaysia maintained full central government territorial control. No significant
armed insurgencies, separatist movements, or foreign military presence challenged
federal authority across all states including Sabah and Sarawak.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2006, Malaysia

2006.assessment:
Full territorial control by central government continued. Malaysia experienced
no armed conflict. The Sulu intrusion into Sabah was not yet a significant issue
at this time; overall territorial integrity maintained.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2007, Malaysia

2007.assessment:
Central government exercised full effective control over Malaysian territory.
The BERSIH 1.0 rally in November 2007 was a domestic political event with no
territorial implications. No insurgent or secessionist threats to territorial integrity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2008, Malaysia

2008.assessment:
Full territorial control by BN government continued despite electoral setbacks
in March 2008 elections (opposition won 5 states). Electoral defeat did not affect
federal government's effective control over all Malaysian territory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2009, Malaysia

2009.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained under Najib Razak who became PM in April 2009.
No armed challenges to government authority. Malaysia remained peaceful with effective
government administration across all 13 states and federal territories.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2010, Malaysia

2010.assessment:
Central government maintained full effective territorial control. No armed insurgency
or separatist activity. Malaysia continued as a stable state with full federal
administration across all regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2011, Malaysia

2011.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. The BERSIH 2.0 rally in July 2011 was a
major political protest but did not challenge territorial integrity. Government
exercised full administrative control over all states.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2012, Malaysia

2012.assessment:
Central government maintained full territorial control. The BERSIH 3.0 rally in
April 2012 further galvanized opposition but had no territorial implications.
All states remained under effective federal administration.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2013, Malaysia

2013.assessment:
Full territorial control continued. The Lahad Datu standoff in February 2013 (armed
Sulu followers entering Sabah from Philippines) was a brief security incident but was
resolved militarily without lasting territorial disruption. BN retained federal government.

sources.citation: ICG, 'The Philippines: The Risks of the 'Bangsamoro Process'', June 2013
Reuters, 'Malaysia army kills 8 Sulu gunmen in Sabah', March 2013

2014.assessment:
Full territorial control by central government. The Lahad Datu aftermath was managed
through enhanced security presence in eastern Sabah. No insurgent groups challenged
government authority in any significant portion of Malaysian territory.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2015, Malaysia

2015.assessment:
Central government exercised full effective territorial control across all Malaysian
territory. The 1MDB scandal dominated politics but did not affect territorial governance.
No armed challenges to federal authority existed.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2016, Malaysia

2016.assessment:
Malaysia under Najib Razak maintained full territorial control. The 1MDB scandal
intensified internationally but did not affect domestic territorial administration.
All states governed effectively under BN federal authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2017, Malaysia

2017.assessment:
Full government territorial control in the lead-up to the 2018 elections.
Electoral boundary redelineation (gerrymandering) was a political controversy
but had no bearing on territorial administration or control.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2018, Malaysia

2018.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained through the historic May 2018 election and
peaceful transfer of power to Pakatan Harapan. The transition was orderly with
no territorial disputes or armed challenges. All 13 states and federal territories
under effective central government administration.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2019, Malaysia

2019.assessment:
Pakatan Harapan government maintained full territorial control. No armed challenges
or secessionist movements. Malaysia remained a stable state with effective
administration across all regions.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2020, Malaysia

2020.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained through the February 2020 Sheraton Move political
crisis, which caused the collapse of the PH government and installation of Muhyiddin
Yassin's Perikatan Nasional government. Political instability did not translate into
any territorial fragmentation or loss of government authority.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2021, Malaysia

2021.assessment:
Full territorial control under Ismail Sabri Yaakob who became PM in August 2021
following Muhyiddin's resignation. Political musical chairs continued but government
maintained effective administration nationwide.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2022, Malaysia

2022.assessment:
Full territorial control maintained. November 2022 elections resulted in a hung
parliament; Anwar Ibrahim eventually formed a unity government. Transition was
peaceful with no territorial security incidents.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2023, Malaysia

2023.assessment:
Full territorial control by Anwar Ibrahim unity government. Malaysia continued
as a stable state with no armed insurgencies or territorial challenges.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2024, Malaysia

2024.assessment:
Malaysia under the Anwar Ibrahim unity government maintained full effective territorial
control over all states and federal territories. No armed challenges or secessionist
movements threatened territorial integrity.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom in the World 2025, Malaysia

2025.assessment: 2025 data not yet published for territorial_control. Malaysia stable; expected to show continuity with 2024 values when released.

2003.assessment: Open Budget Survey did not exist in 2003. IBP published its first OBS in 2006. Malaysia had a functional budget process under Abdullah Ahmad Badawi, but no formal IBP score is available for this year.

2004.assessment: Open Budget Survey did not exist in 2004. IBP published its first OBS in 2006. Malaysia had a functional budget process under Abdullah Ahmad Badawi, but no formal IBP score is available for this year.

2005.assessment: Open Budget Survey did not exist in 2005. IBP published its first OBS in 2006. Malaysia had a functional budget process under Abdullah Ahmad Badawi, but no formal IBP score is available for this year.

2006.assessment:
First OBS edition scored Malaysia at 39 — minimal transparency. Budget documents
published but limited detail. Petronas revenues not included in budget. No
independent fiscal institutions. Minimal public participation mechanisms.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia

2007.assessment:
Estimated 39 (same as 2006 OBS score) for 2007 — no survey in this year.
Budget transparency environment unchanged. OBS 2008 to follow.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2006/2008, interpolated for 2007

2008.assessment:
OBS 2008 scored Malaysia at 39 — unchanged from 2006. Budget transparency
remained minimal. Budget published but limited public access to comprehensive
fiscal information.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia

2009.assessment:
Interpolated estimate of 39 for 2009 (between 2008 and 2010 OBS).
Budget transparency environment broadly unchanged.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2008/2010, interpolated for 2009

2010.assessment:
OBS 2010 scored Malaysia 44 — slight improvement. Still limited transparency
but some improvement in budget document availability. Petronas revenues
disclosure limited.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia

2011.assessment: Interpolated estimate of 44 for 2011 (between 2010 and 2012 OBS).

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2010/2012, interpolated for 2011

2012.assessment:
OBS 2012 scored Malaysia 39 — regression to 2006/2008 levels. Limited budget
transparency. Limited access to comprehensive fiscal information.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia

2013.assessment: Interpolated estimate of 39. Budget transparency environment broadly unchanged.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012/2015, interpolated for 2013

2014.assessment:
Interpolated estimate; 1MDB off-balance-sheet dealings represent serious
budget transparency failure even if OBS scored this period similarly to prior years.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2012/2015, interpolated for 2014

2015.assessment:
OBS 2015 scored Malaysia 46 — limited transparency. Some improvement in budget
document availability. However 1MDB off-budget dealings represented major actual
fiscal opacity not captured by OBS methodology.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia

2016.assessment:
Interpolated estimate. Budget transparency formally minimal to limited but
1MDB scandal demonstrated severe practical opacity in sovereign wealth fund
management.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2015/2017, interpolated for 2016

2017.assessment:
OBS 2017 scored Malaysia 48 — limited transparency; marginal improvement.
Budget documents published; some improvement in access. 1MDB fiscal opacity
in parallel.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2017, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia
IBP Open Budget Survey 2017/2019, interpolated for 2018

2019.assessment:
OBS 2019 scored Malaysia 46. Limited to moderate transparency. PH government
improved fiscal disclosure but OBS methodology has lags and doesn't capture
all reform progress.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia

2020.assessment:
Interpolated estimate. Political instability (Sheraton Move) and COVID-19 may
have reduced transparency in supplemental budget processes.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2019/2021, interpolated for 2020

2021.assessment:
OBS 2021 scored Malaysia 48. Limited transparency. Emergency period may have
reduced parliamentary oversight of COVID-19 spending.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021, Open Budget Index score for Malaysia

2022.assessment:
Interpolated estimate. Anwar unity government committed to fiscal transparency
reforms. Budget reform agenda begun.

sources.citation: IBP Open Budget Survey 2021/2023, interpolated for 2022

2023.assessment:
OBS 2023 scored Malaysia 55 — substantial transparency; significant improvement.
Anwar government's fiscal reforms improving budget transparency. More detailed
budget documents, improved public access.

2003.assessment:
Press severely restricted under Mahathir's government. Print media required
annual licensing; major newspapers owned by BN parties. Printing Presses and
Publications Act (PPPA) gave government power to revoke licenses. Online media
(Malaysiakini) emerging but under legal pressure.

sources.citation: Freedom House, Freedom of the Press 2004, Malaysia

2004.assessment:
Press continued under PPPA licensing regime. Some relaxation under Badawi
with Malaysiakini allowed to operate. Mainstream media still controlled by
BN-linked ownership. Problematic press environment.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2004/2005

2005.assessment:
Press freedom constrained by PPPA and BN-linked media ownership. Internet
expanding as alternative information source with less formal restriction.
RSF ranked Malaysia around 110-130th out of ~167 countries in this period.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2006

2006.assessment:
Continuing press restrictions. Mainstream media under BN control. Bloggers
and online media growing but subject to defamation and sedition laws. Malaysia
ranked approximately 124th by RSF.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2007

2007.assessment:
Press freedom continued to be severely restricted. BERSIH coverage in mainstream
media censored. Bloggers targeted with defamation suits. RSF ranked Malaysia
around 124th.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2008

2008.assessment:
Online media played pivotal role in opposition's 2008 election gains. Traditional
media still controlled by BN but internet free relatively. RSF around 132nd out
of ~175.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2009

2009.assessment:
Press environment continued under PPPA constraints. Online media growing.
RSF ranking approximately 131st.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2010

2010.assessment:

bloggers and journalists. Print media licensing continued.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2011

2011.assessment:
Press freedom constrained. Coverage of BERSIH protests censored in mainstream
media. Online independent journalism growing. RSF ranked Malaysia around 122nd.

Press environment continued restrictive. 1MDB pre-history. Online media

2013.assessment:
RSF score 38.8 (higher = more free on legacy scale, but 2013 methodology context:
Malaysia ranked 145th out of 179 countries). Press covered 2013 elections under
constraints. Mainstream media heavily BN-biased. Online media providing alternative.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2013, score for Malaysia

2014.assessment:
Press remained restricted. Sedition Act used to silence journalists and bloggers.
RSF ranked Malaysia around 147th. Several journalists and editors prosecuted.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2015

2015.assessment:
Press freedom significantly worsened in context of 1MDB crackdown. The Edge
Media Group (broke 1MDB story) suspended temporarily. Journalists self-censored.
RSF ranked Malaysia 147th — deteriorating environment.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2016

2016.assessment:
Press freedom deteriorated further. Anti-fake news legislation prepared. Online
media (Malaysiakini, Malaysia Chronicle) continued operating under threat.
RSF ranked Malaysia around 146th.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2017

2017.assessment:
One of the worst periods for press freedom pre-2018. Anti-Fake News Act being
prepared. Online news sites under harassment. RSF ranked Malaysia around 144th.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2018

2018.assessment:
RSF 2019 index (covering 2018 situation) scored Malaysia 30.0 — ranking 123rd.
Significant improvement after May 2018 regime change. PH government repealed
Anti-Fake News Act; released political prisoners; media licensing reviewed.
Press freedom measurably improved in H2 2018. Full-year score still dragged
by pre-May situation.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2019, score for Malaysia

2019.assessment:
RSF 2020 (2019 data) scored Malaysia 36.3 — 101st of 180 countries. Under PH
government, media freedom improved substantially. New media emerged; journalists
investigated government without immediate retaliation. Problematic conditions
remained but significant improvement from pre-2018.

sources.citation: RSF World Press Freedom Index 2020, score for Malaysia

2020.assessment:
Press freedom slightly deteriorated after Sheraton Move installed PN government.
New government less press-friendly than PH; some journalists targeted. RSF around
119th (2021 index). Still improved from pre-2018 baseline.
